module: Module 12: AP Exam Synthesis and Free-Response Strategy

sections.text: AP Calculus AB. Module Twelve — exam synthesis and free response strategy. This is the last module. No new calculus today. Just everything you already know, organized the way the AP exam will test you. Two days before the championship. This is the film session.

True story from the AP Reading. A student solved three free response parts perfectly. Got every number right. Scored zero on all three. Part A — missing units. Part B — wrote the answer without the word BECAUSE. Part C — gave the calculator's number without writing the setup integral. Right math. Wrong rubric. This module is about not being that student.

Three things. One — how the exam is actually built. Two sections, ninety minutes each, fifty-fifty weighted. Two — the six free response patterns that show up every single year. If you've seen them before, the exam feels short. Three — the rubric: units, the word BECAUSE, the setup integral, and how to use the calculator without losing precision.

The exam. Two sections, three hours fifteen minutes total. Section One — forty-five multiple choice, one hundred five minutes, fifty percent of your score. Part A: thirty problems in sixty minutes, no calculator. Part B: fifteen problems in forty-five minutes, calculator required. Section Two — six free response, ninety minutes, the other fifty percent. Part A: two problems, thirty minutes, calculator. Part B: four problems, sixty minutes, no calculator. Notice the rhythm. Most of your calculator time goes to a small number of problems where the math actually needs it.

Six patterns. They rotate, but they show up every year. One — rate in, rate out. Water flows in, water flows out, what's in the tank at time T. Two — particle motion. Three — area and volume, from Modules Ten and Eleven. Four — table based. They give you a table of values and ask for a trapezoidal sum, or a justification using the Mean Value or Intermediate Value Theorem. Five — differential equations, separable, with an initial condition and a slope field. Six — function defined by an integral. Memorize the patterns. The first move on any FRQ is just naming which one it is.

Pattern one. Water flows in at R of t, out at S of t, starts with V naught gallons. Volume at time capital T equals V naught plus the integral from zero to capital T of R of t minus S of t, dee t. Write the setup. Always write the setup. Even if the calculator does the integral, the setup is what earns the point. Final units — gallons. Always.

Pattern two. Particle motion. Same example from Module Ten — velocity equals t squared minus four on zero to three. Net displacement, the integral of v, equals negative three. Total distance, the integral of the absolute value of v, equals twenty-three thirds. The AP exam will ask for both. In the same problem. If you write the same number twice, you lose a point. Same trip. Different question.

Pattern six. g of x equals the integral from a to x of f of t, dee t. By the Fundamental Theorem, g prime of x equals f of x. So if they give you the graph of f, you can describe g without ever computing the integral. g has a maximum where f changes from positive to negative — BECAUSE g prime equals f, and the sign change is from plus to minus. Memorize that sentence.

Your turn. Let g of x equal the integral from zero to x of f of t, dee t. Suppose f is positive on the open interval zero to three, and negative on the open interval three to five. Where does g have its maximum on the closed interval zero to five — and JUSTIFY with the word BECAUSE. Pause the video. Write the sentence. Press play when you're ready.

Walking through it. g has its maximum at x equals three, BECAUSE g prime of x equals f of x by the Fundamental Theorem, and f changes from positive to negative at x equals three. That sentence has three rubric pieces — the where, the BECAUSE, and the citation of the Fundamental Theorem. Skip any one of them and the point is gone. Write the full sentence every time.

The single biggest score recovery technique on this entire exam. Wrong column — the student writes g has a max at x equals three. True statement. Zero points. It's a conclusion without a reason. Right column — g has a max at x equals three BECAUSE g prime equals f, and f changes from positive to negative at three. Same conclusion. Full point. The word BECAUSE is the magic word. If you can't think of anything else, write BECAUSE and then explain. The graders are trained to look for it.

Three calculator rules. One — when the calculator gives you an intermediate value, store it with the arrow key. Do not re-type. Re-typing zero point seven three two, you'll round, you'll drop a digit, and your final answer is off in the third decimal. Two — final numerical answers on the calculator section must be at least three decimal places. Two decimals is not enough. Three — always write the setup integral before pressing the button. The calculator answer alone earns zero. The setup plus the answer earns full points.

Units. Every applied free response answer needs units. Gallons. Meters per second. Dollars per day. If R of t is in gallons per minute and you integrate over minutes, the answer is in gallons — the units of f times the units of t. One missing unit is one missing point. Every time. Train yourself: when you draw the box around your answer, the number AND the units go in the box.

Time budget. Ninety minutes, six free response problems — fifteen minutes per problem. But here's the trick. Do parts a and b of every problem first. They're almost always the easy entries — set up the integral, identify the value, plug in a number. Then come back for parts c and d, the justification heavy ones. Circle every final answer so the grader sees it. And if you have two minutes left on a blank part — write the setup integral anyway. Partial credit is real. A setup with no answer often scores one of two points.

Recap. Exam structure — two sections, fifty-fifty, free response Part A is two problems with the calculator in thirty minutes, Part B is four problems no calculator in sixty. Six FRQ patterns — name the pattern first. Always write the setup integral. Always include units. Always use the word BECAUSE on justifications. Store intermediate values on the calculator and round only the final answer, to at least three decimals.

How to actually master this module. There is no next module. This is the last lesson. So your next step is real practice. Pull a recent released AP Calculus AB free response set from The College Board's website. Set a ninety minute timer. Sit it like the real thing — calculator only on Part A. Use Khan Academy's AP Calc AB practice course for the timed multiple choice. Book an advisor session through the GIIS dashboard for a full timed mock exam, scored against the official rubric. The final week, target your three weakest patterns from that mock. You're ready. Go earn the five.
